pattern: Reducing a pairwise-intersection threshold rule to a graph lets connected-component counting via union-find answer the grouping question directly.
hints: Convert each property list into a set so 'distinct integers in common' becomes a plain set intersection.
Build an edge between every pair of indices whose intersection size reaches k, then the question becomes counting connected components.
Use union-find (or a visited-based traversal): union every qualifying pair, then count how many distinct roots (or unvisited starting points) remain.

edge_inputs.description: single node graph is always one component
duplicate values inside one array must count once toward intersection size
k equal to the maximum possible intersection so only identical sets connect
no pair ever reaches the threshold, every node isolated
all nodes fully connected through shared values

stress.description: random small property lists over a narrow value range to force many intersections
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    m = rng.randint(1, 20)
    properties = [[rng.randint(1, 15) for _ in range(m)] for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, m)
    return [properties, k]
